---
title: "Client Report - Exploring Names"
subtitle: "Unit 1 Task 1"
author: "Camila Rojas"
format:
  html:
    self-contained: true
    page-layout: full
    title-block-banner: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: body
    number-sections: false
    html-math-method: katex
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show the code"
    code-overflow: wrap
    code-copy: hover
    code-tools:
        source: false
        toggle: true
        caption: See code
execute: 
  warning: false
    
---

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from lets_plot import *

LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)
```

```{python}
# Learn morea about Code Cells: https://quarto.org/docs/reference/cells/cells-jupyter.html

df = pd.read_csv("https://github.com/byuidatascience/data4names/raw/master/data-raw/names_year/names_year.csv")

df.head()
df.columns
```

## QUESTION 1

__What was the earliest year that the name ‘Felisha’ was used?__

After filtering the dataset for the name Felisha, I looked for the earliest year it showed. The earliest year the name Felisha was used in the dataset is 1964.

```{python}
# Q1

df.query('name == "Felisha"')["year"].min()

```


## QUESTION 2

__What year had the most babies named 'David'? How many babies were named 'David' that year?__

I filtered the data to only include the name David and then looked at how many babies were named David each year. After sorting the results, I found that 1955 was the year with the most babies named David, with about 64,754 babies.

```{python}
# Q2

david = df.query("name == 'David'")

david.sort_values("Total", ascending=False).head(1)[["year","Total"]]

```


## QUESTION 3

__What year did your name hit its peak? How many babies were named your name in that year?__

I filtered the dataset for my name, Camila, and then sorted the data by the total number of babies. The name Camila reached its highest popularity in 2015, with about 5,240 babies that year.

```{python}
# Q3

camila = df.query("name == 'Camila'")

camila.sort_values("Total", ascending=False).head(1)[["year","Total"]]

```



## QUESTION 4

__How many babies are named ‘Oliver’ in the state of Utah for all years?__  

I filtered the dataset to only include the name Oliver and then added up all the values from the Utah column across all years. In total, there were about 1,704 babies named Oliver in Utah.

```{python}
# Q4

df.query('name == "Oliver"')["UT"].sum()

```

---

## QUESTION 5

__In the most recent year, what was the most common female name in Utah?__ 

I first found the most recent year in the dataset and then filtered the data to only include that year. After sorting the Utah values from highest to lowest, I found that William was the most common name in Utah, with 309 babies. The dataset doesn't separate names by gender, so this result is based on total name counts.

```{python}
# Q5

most_recent_year = df["year"].max()

utah_data = df[df["year"]== most_recent_year]

utah_data.sort_values("UT", ascending=False)[["year","name","UT"]].head(10)

```


---
